era_context: The late 14th century: Ming China consolidating Han rule; Ottoman expansion in the Balkans; Western European kingdoms in dynastic conflict; the Great Schism fragmenting papal authority; the Timurid Empire in Central Asia.

events.title: Henry III becomes King of Castile
events.description: Henry III (r. 1390–1406) became King of Castile in 1390, succeeding his father John I. Henry III was a young king who inherited a kingdom torn by factional conflicts and noble rivalries. His reign saw the consolidation of royal authority and the rise of powerful noble families, particularly the Mendoza family. Henry III was known for his efforts to reform the Castilian state and to expand royal power at the expense of the nobility. His reign was a critical period in the development of the Spanish state, as Castile was gradually becoming the dominant power in the Iberian Peninsula.

sources.name: Castilian chronicles (Pérez de Guzmán, Generaciones y Semblanzas)

events.certainty_note: Henry III's accession to the throne of Castile in 1390 is precisely dated in Castilian sources.

events.title: Bayezid I becomes Ottoman Sultan
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), became Ottoman Sultan in 1389 following the death of his father Murad I at the Battle of Kosovo. By 1390, Bayezid had consolidated his position against internal rivals and was beginning his rapid expansion of Ottoman territory. His reign saw the transformation of the Ottoman state into a major regional power, with significant territorial gains in Anatolia and the Balkans. Bayezid was known for his military prowess and his aggressive expansion, earning him the epithet 'Thunderbolt.' His reign would last until 1402, when he was defeated by Timur at the Battle of Ankara.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's accession to the Ottoman throne in 1389 is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources. By 1390, he had consolidated his position.

events.title: John V Palaiologus dies; Manuel II becomes Byzantine Emperor
events.description: John V Palaiologus, the Byzantine Emperor, died on 16 February 1391 (not 1390; see disconfirming evidence). However, 1390 marks the final year of his reign and the anticipation of succession. John V had ruled for a long period (with interruptions) and had witnessed the decline of the Byzantine Empire. His death would be followed by the accession of his son Manuel II, who would rule during the critical period of Ottoman siege and expansion. John V's reign was marked by efforts to preserve the Byzantine state in the face of Ottoman expansion and internal factional conflicts.
events.certainty_note: John V died on 16 February 1391, not 1390. This entry marks 1390 as the final year of his reign.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming power; Confucian orthodoxy restored
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, r. 1368–1398) was consolidating Ming power and restoring Confucian orthodoxy in the early 1390s. The Ming Dynasty had replaced the Yuan Mongols and was establishing a new Chinese state based on Han Chinese culture and Confucian principles. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule and his efforts to eliminate Mongol influence and restore Han Chinese cultural authority. His reign saw the restoration of the civil service examination system based on Confucian classics, the compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia (begun 1403), and the construction of major architectural projects like the Forbidden City (begun 1406). By 1390, the Ming state was administratively consolidated and culturally confident.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of Ming power and restoration of Confucian orthodoxy are well-documented in Ming official records.

events.title: Great Schism continues; rival popes claim legitimacy
events.description: The Great Schism (1378–1417) continued unabated in 1390, with rival popes in Rome and Avignon claiming legitimacy. The schism was a major crisis for the Catholic Church, fragmenting papal authority and creating confusion among the faithful. In 1390, Boniface IX was the Roman Pope and Clement VII was the Avignon Pope. Efforts to resolve the schism through councils and diplomatic pressure were ongoing but unsuccessful. The schism would not be resolved until the Council of Constance (1414–1418), which would establish a single pope and reform the Church's governance.
events.certainty_note: The Great Schism and the identities of the rival popes in 1390 are well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records.

disconfirming_evidence: John V Palaiologus died on 16 February 1391, not 1390. This entry has been corrected to reflect this. The significance of 1390 as a turning point in Byzantine history is thus somewhat overstated; the actual transition occurred in 1391.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1390 is dominated by European, Islamic, and East Asian written sources. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent. The sources are predominantly later compilations rather than contemporary records, introducing dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives. The year 1390 marks several important political transitions (Henry III in Castile, Bayezid I in the Ottoman Empire, and the final year of John V in Byzantium), making it a significant year in European and Mediterranean history.

graph_edges.note: Henry III's accession in 1390 began a reign that would last until 1406
Bayezid I's consolidation of Ottoman power in 1390 led to his confrontation with Timur in 1402
John V's death in 1391 led to Manuel II's accession
The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of Ming power in 1390 set the stage for succession disputes after his death in 1398
The unresolved Great Schism of 1390 led to the Council of Constance in 1414
